text:
https://www.dailyprincetonian.com/article/2026/03/princeton-news-adpol-proctoring-honor-code-in-person-exams

it's understated the extent to which academic integrity rot is the consequence of college becoming more about vocational training than holistic education, in the liberal arts tradition.

to say something obvious: people enforce norms they want enforced generally. i suspect that the majority of today's college students -- even at top schools -- do not want academic rigor. it is not instrumental to their goals! on net, it's an obstacle.

i resent this. i quite enjoyed school, and i was good at it. i also appreciated the person it made me become, the knowledge for its own sake. i did not like managing a complicated schedule and extracurricular activities. and i really did not like trading in optionality for success. it was a violation of the contract that had guided my pre-adult life: that education was paramount, that at the end of my education the institutions would sort me meritocratically. stories of the brightest gen xers having a proper college experience caring deeply about their studies, studying english or something, spending a year or two in europe post-grad before eventually matriculating to a good job at a prestigious company. they did not do an "internship."

and to really indulge now, we should consider which sorts of people are rwarded under the current paradigm. it is not those with the greatest potential -- these folks will preserve their optionality. but rather it's those who would lose under the traditional sorting mechanism -- if you are a poor student, a good strategy is to give up on your classes and bet it all on some niche in the labor market. the equilibrium here is obvious, and i think it's probably worse than a world where no college student is allowed to work before their 22nd birthday.

it's difficult to conceptualize any sort of policy proposal around this that isn't comlpetely communist, though. of course, companies prefer to hire new grads with experience because it derisks their employment. it also means that the company has to invest less in training. it's possible that companies were only willing to invest substantially in job training due to 20th century white collar company loyalty - which, as far as i can tell, was mostly a cultural norm or consequence of poor labor market effiency rather than a particular policy. if you forbade new grads from acquiring work experience during school, many companies might correctly determine that most employees will leave before the company gets a return on its investment in their professional training, so they will stop hiring new grads. unless employees are willing to sign a long-term contract with the employer, which would be awful in new ways and maybe much worse on the whole.

i want to make one concession: there are many good, virtuous things that the current paradigm differentially rewards. specifically: agency, the ability to navigate complex systems, and the sort of social/macroeconomic literacy required to strategize in a game with dynamic rewards, rules, actors.
title: princeton honor code

text:
don't like parallelism, vibes are off

really nice that we're doing agi with autoregressive models
title: parallel processing

text: the sam kriss piece "child's play" in harper's was very entertaining, pretty funny. and the critical perspective on "agency" is timely. but he's very smug all the way through-- one imagines sam the proverbial child in the back of the classroom, snickering while their peer reads their paper, feigning ignorance when the teacher calls upon them "do you have something to say?" -- so ultimately i don't like it, in the sense that i don't think it would be good for more things like it to be written and published.
title: sam kriss in harpers

text: finally, a model that hurts your feelings
title: opus 4.6

text: work is quite fun now, but hyperventilating at the terminal all day does not feel like an equilibrium. i hope that in the future, maybe soon, i can move slow and focus deeply again
title: 100 year mental health break

text:
after i drink coffee in the morning, if i don't immediately start working, i have ideas and start penning little essays in my head

these start as drafts of twitter posts. but i never post them, for two reasons i think: (1) too many people follow me, and some of these people are impressive, and i don't want to show half-baked / poorly written thoughts to these people; (2) i have trouble being sincere online

but i would still like a place to post unpolished thoughts

if it's not public, then i don't find it exciting, and i won't write things down

so here we are
title: meta

text:
one way san francisco is different from new york is that you can sit down in a cafe and it doesnt feel like you're getting away with something

stepping outside in new york is war. in sf it's just getting breakfast

taking the bart from sf to berkeley was quite simple, but i was surprised how much it cost

chatted with a random guy waiting for the next pick up game at ymca. he was a swe intern. he brought up "tech twitter" within 3 turns of conversation

surprised how some parts resembled any other city
title: sf
nba tanking
text:
its remarkable that nba lottery system has been around this long without things getting _that_ bad

under certain conditions, teams are rewarded for losing games. of course they will intentionally lose games! incentives are broken

its more fundamental than that though. teams are only indirectly rewarded for winning games through better tv deals, demand for games, merchandise sales. i wager team revenue increasingly decouples from winning games over time because of celebrity players

this might happen with lebron's final season, has arguably already happened with lebron on the lakers. his farewell tour will do crazy ticket sales, and he will be probably a ~0 VORP player, but wouldn't be surprised if teams would trade substantial assets to grab the revenue.

eventually there will be a larger reckoning similar about misaligned incentives. nba will have a choice then: have teams participate in revenue sharing, or become a pure entertainment league a la savannah bananas. 50/50 either way i think.

im cautiously optimistic nba will do team revenue sharing (s.t. teams are rewarded for winning games, playoff performance) because of gambling partnerships and money prize for nba cup. seems like nba has broken through a lot of the taboos around money/competing for money/financial incentives for people who are already rich
